reliable under increasing data volume and traffic load
Observability and Monitoring of the Platform Itself
The platform is not just a monitoring tool for external systems—it must also
monitor itself with the same rigor, depth, and reliability
If the platform is not observable, its outputs cannot be trusted.
Failures can occur at multiple layers:
provide real-time visibility into system health
enable fast debugging and root cause analysis
ensure trust in data freshness and correctness
The system should be built on three pillars:
Number of pending jobs in queues.
increasing queue depth → workers insufficient or failing
Time taken to process a job from ingestion to completion.
Number and rate of failed jobs.
Time between webhook arrival and processing start/completion.
Time taken to serve dashboard API responses.
Percentage of requests served from cache.
Failed login or API authentication attempts.
All metrics should be sliceable by:
Track a request as it flows through multiple services.
Webhook → Ingestion → Queue → Worker → Transform → DB → KPI
Unique ID assigned to each request or event.
track a single event across services
All logs must include tenant/project context.
Ensure data is entering the system correctly.
which tenants/projects have the most errors
Include tenant, project, and connector metadata.
Observability is what turns a system from
“If you can’t observe it, you can’t trust it.”

The frontend is the primary value surface of the platform. Even if the backend architecture is strong, the product will fail if users cannot quickly understand what is happening, what needs attention, and what actions to take. The UX architecture must therefore translate a highly complex system into a clear, role-aware, and low-friction experience.
The UI must serve multiple user types at once:
super admins managing tenants and platform health
project admins onboarding and configuring integrations
operations users monitoring failures and sync states
business users tracking performance and trends
technical users investigating logs and mismatches
viewer roles consuming dashboards without configuration access
That means the frontend must balance:
The UX goals below define the principles for achieving that balance.
One of the most important goals of the frontend is to make onboarding feel guided, predictable, and low effort.
The interface should reduce ambiguity during setup by:
The product’s value depends on how quickly a new project can become operational. If onboarding feels technical, confusing, or error-prone, adoption slows and implementation burden rises.
separate essential setup from optional advanced settings
present integration health and setup readiness clearly
The frontend should make the platform feel like a product, not like an implementation service.
The second major UX goal is speed of understanding. A user landing on the dashboard should be able to answer key questions within seconds:
Is something urgent happening right now?
This is a monitoring product. Its value is not only in storing data, but in reducing the time between signal and decision.
clear distinction between status, trends, and anomalies
Users should not need to dig through multiple pages to understand current state.
While top-level summaries are essential, the platform must also support deep operational investigation. Users need to go from a red alert or KPI anomaly to the underlying cause quickly.
access to sync logs and webhook states
traceability from summary view to raw evidence
A monitoring platform that only shows high-level metrics but cannot explain them will not be trusted during incidents.
Every important summary element should answer:
Drill-down should feel like a natural continuation of monitoring, not a separate technical subsystem.

The platform supports users with very different goals. A business user tracking revenue should not be forced to navigate low-level sync data by default, and an operator troubleshooting a broken connector should not have to interpret executive revenue dashboards to find the issue.
Mixing all data into a single undifferentiated interface creates confusion, slows users down, and increases perceived complexity.
consistent language depending on the user’s goal
The product should feel tailored, even if it is built on shared underlying data.
Many users of the platform will not be engineers. They may be project managers, operations
, e-commerce managers, analysts, or client stakeholders. The UX must therefore reduce technical complexity wherever possible without hiding critical truth.
use clear, business-friendly terminology where possible
explain technical states in understandable language
avoid exposing raw internal concepts by default
use visuals and status indicators effectively
reveal technical detail progressively when needed
If the UI is too technical, adoption drops. If it is too abstract, trust drops. The platform must bridge that gap carefully.
instead of only showing “webhook delivery failure,” show “real-time updates are failing”
instead of “queue backlog critical,” show “data processing delayed”
combine technical detail with human-readable interpretation
This allows non-technical users to remain informed without needing system-level expertise.
The product should be organized into a set of clearly defined UI areas that reflect the primary user workflows. These areas should feel coherent, role-aware, and easy to navigate.

keep access flows fast and secure
clearly communicate errors such as invalid credentials or expired sessions
support enterprise-ready authentication patterns over time

This area sets the first impression of the product and should feel trustworthy and professional.
This is the entry point for setting up a new project and should function as a guided workflow rather than a generic admin form.
This area is central to the product’s low-touch onboarding promise.

give users full visibility into what is connected
make it easy to understand integration health
allow controlled editing without overwhelming the user
action panel for validate, resync, disconnect
should feel like a reliable operations workspace, not just a settings page.

help identify where attention is needed
This dashboard should answer: “Where across the portfolio do we need to focus right now?”

give users one central overview for the project
blend business and operational signals intelligently
allow quick transition into detail pages
This is the core daily-use surface for most users and should be optimized for speed of understanding.
These views provide deeper business analysis beyond the summary dashboard.
customer and conversion insights where available
allow filtering and breakdown by channel, source, and time
These pages should help business users move from “what is happening” to “why it is happening.”
This is the dedicated view for monitoring connected systems and pipeline reliability.
separate healthy vs degraded vs failed states clearly
support proactive monitoring and operational response
direct actions like revalidate or resync
This area is especially important for operations and support teams.
These are investigation-oriented views for tracing what happened inside the platform.
make technical detail accessible without becoming unusable
filter by date, connector, status, severity
These views should support both day-to-day debugging and formal audit review.
is the platform’s triage and response interface.
This area contains the administrative controls needed to manage the project or tenant safely.
clearly separate advanced settings from routine actions
This area should feel structured and controlled, not overloaded.
To serve different user intents effectively, the dashboard experience should be structured into three conceptual layers:
These are not necessarily separate products. They may exist as tabs, modes, routes, or dashboard tiers within the same platform. The goal is to match interface structure to user intent.
The Executive View is designed for rapid business understanding and high-level decision support.
keep the view concise but meaningful
major alert banner or summary panel

This view should be readable in minutes and useful in meetings, reviews, and daily business check-ins.
The Operational View is designed for teams who need to ensure the system is functioning correctly on an ongoing basis.
expose what is broken, delayed, or degraded
support active monitoring during the day

This view should prioritize operational signal over business storytelling.
The Investigation View is designed for root-cause analysis and deep troubleshooting.
help users move from symptoms to evidence
expose raw and canonical data relationships
support traceability across jobs, events, and records

still structured enough to avoid overwhelming the user.
